title: Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors

narrative.executive_view: This is a durable-demand physical-services business, but not a large AI labor-arbitrage business. AI can take friction out of the office, selling and estimating stack; it cannot replace licensed technicians who diagnose, install and repair equipment at a building. The strongest buy-and-build case is recurring residential/light-commercial service, with caution on entry price and construction exposure. [S1][S2]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The practical near-term tools are call handling, booking, dispatch, CRM updates, estimate follow-up, job summaries and marketing—not autonomous wrench work. ServiceTitan's contractor survey finds AI use concentrated in sales content, qualification and CRM work, while EPA certification remains required for refrigerant work. I therefore put high within-role exposure in clerical work and modest exposure in field roles. [S1][S2]
narrative.value_capture: Flat-rate service menus and memberships can let a well-run local operator retain part of lower administrative cost, but customer acquisition, local competition and commercial bidding pass some savings through. The stated capture estimate deliberately excludes the technician/licensing floor: that floor protects demand, not the share of office savings retained.
narrative.adoption_timing: AI is already visible in contractor software, but effective adoption means integrated workflows with clean price-book, CRM and dispatch data. The commercial contractor survey reports 29% use of AI agents, below the 50% boundary, so this packet uses a positive—not zero—time to 50% effective adoption. [S2]
narrative.consolidation_economics: There are active strategic and sponsor-backed acquirers in construction services, but public-platform trading multiples are not small-shop transaction multiples. BizBuySell's sold HVAC businesses average 2.75x seller discretionary earnings; its universe is mainly local owner-operated businesses. A scaled, recurring-service exit can warrant more, but the exit estimate remains well below public construction-services trading marks. [S3][S4]
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE. A customer still needs physical diagnosis, installation and repair, and EPA Section 608 requires certification for covered refrigerant work. Smart equipment and remote diagnostics can reduce some visits or shift relationship ownership, but they do not turn a text model into a field technician. [S1]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The NAICS code mixes residential service, commercial mechanical contracting and new construction, while the supplied labor and target-count inputs are broad proxies. AI can also improve competitors' price books and lead conversion. The crucial uncertainty is how much 20–99 employee residential-service activity belongs in the EBITDA band, not whether AI replaces trade labor.

sources.title: Section 608 Technician Certification | US EPA
sources.what_it_supports: EPA says covered refrigeration and air-conditioning technicians must be certified and pass an EPA-approved test.

sources.title: ServiceTitan Commercial Sales and Marketing Report
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 survey of over 1,000 primarily mechanical/electrical/plumbing commercial leaders: 29% used AI agents; current uses include content, lead qualification and CRM updates.

sources.title: HVAC Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks
sources.what_it_supports: Reported 2021–25 sold HVAC businesses averaged 2.75x owner earnings; sold-business earnings multiple quartiles were 1.99x, 2.58x, 2.75x and 3.33x.

sources.title: Capstone Partners Construction Services M&A Coverage Report, August 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Public construction-services index showed 18.9x average LTM EBITDA; it is a public-company comparator, not small HVAC deal evidence.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23822 ($100,763,014,046) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23822 ($306,277,104,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23822. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades and Extraction Workers
First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $260,272 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238220); class <5: 63,588 firms, avg revenue/firm $386,166 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,739 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,175 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,348,756 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12,459 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,960,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,643 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9,415 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,892,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $787,462 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,152 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,865,618 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,969,303 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 215 firms, avg revenue/firm $311,824,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,821,217 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,152

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI exposure is concentrated in office, sales support and reporting; physical service, installation and supervised field work remain human tasks.
caveats: Role mix is 5-digit proxy and residual is broad.
Certification is evidence about refrigerant tasks, not every plumbing task.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical pipe work dominates; documentation and diagnostic assistance are a small slice.

breakdown_by_role.role: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Installation/service is physical; AI supports manuals and diagnostics.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades and Extraction Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, reporting and job costing automate, while site control persists.
Routine clerical, invoicing and paperwork are highly workflow-automatable.
Reporting and planning assist, while accountability and relationships remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Administrative coordination automates more than field supervision.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Lead qualification and follow-up automate; in-home selling remains human.
Unlisted tail includes estimators, dispatch and back office plus physical trades; use a mixed conservative judgment.

pi_dist.rationale: Menu pricing and memberships retain part of administrative savings, offset by local competition and lead-cost pressure.
caveats: Residential versus commercial revenue mix varies materially.

pi_moat.rationale: Local reputation, memberships and licensed execution protect some savings, but software diffuses.
caveats: Licensing supports demand but is not itself a pricing moat.

t50_years.rationale: Only 29% of surveyed commercial leaders report AI agents, so full effective adoption is below 50% today.
caveats: Vendor-sponsored survey; AI agent use is not the same as effective firm-wide automation.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: ServiceTitan reports 29% currently use AI agents.
caveats: Commercial MEP survey rather than exact NAICS census.

historical_analogs.value: Field-service SaaS/CRM rollout: software first removes dispatch, CRM and sales-administration friction, then diffuses through standardized workflows while field execution remains physical.
historical_analogs.rationale: Current use cases are workflow tools, supporting this analogy.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: Aging-owner succession is a plausible small-business mechanism but no code-specific owner-age census was reopened.
caveats: Not an observed NAICS owner-age statistic.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No directly reopened code-specific succession-shortage study was found; do not claim confirmation.

active_consolidators.rationale: Construction-services report supports an active sponsor/strategic market; count is a conservative US home-services-platform estimate.
caveats: Not a census of 238220 and consolidators are used only for B/M context.

buy_mult.rationale: Uses the 2.75x sold HVAC owner-earnings average as a small-shop anchor, rounded for a $1–10M EBITDA target after owner-comp normalization.
caveats: BizBuySell reports SDE/owner earnings, not normalized EBITDA; larger targets can price higher.

exit_mult.rationale: Scaled recurring-service exit is estimated above local SDE transactions but far below the 18.9x public construction-services reference.
caveats: No direct small-platform HVAC EBITDA exit series was reopened.

pricing_structure.value: Residential service/replacement commonly uses quoted menu/flat-rate work and memberships; commercial work is competitively bid, so administrative savings are partly passed through.
pricing_structure.rationale: Sources describe service, maintenance, repair/install and sales/CRM workflows; pricing mix remains a target-specific diligence question.
caveats: Exact price-book share is not sourced.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Moderate V with a three-year effective-adoption estimate is consistent with workflow automation, not immediate replacement of technicians.

terminal_value.justification: Licensed physical repair/install work persists; AI changes administration and diagnostics rather than performing the on-site service. [S1]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: labor share is ancestor-level/LOW and the code spans heterogeneous contractor subsegments; these are disclosed rather than treated as precise.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI savings face pass-through in competitive commercial bids and through lead/platform competition; retain only a partial share in pi_dist.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share is 23822 ancestor-level with a vintage mismatch and LOW quality; n_band uses a 7.96% broad construction margin that likely understates residential-service targets.
sensitivity: Higher service EBITDA margins would increase the true $1–10M target count.
Higher equipment/material mix would lower effective labor savings.

market_boundary.selection_basis: It is the most relevant repeat-service roll-up target; association/vendor evidence does not cover every NAICS component.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Exit multiple is the largest uncertainty in M.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 1,152 provided; likely materially higher under service margins

reviewer_note: Physical-trades anchor: do not convert licensed human field labor into AI substitution. All score-driving URLs above were reopened on the run date; source S4 is explicitly a public-company proxy, and the unsourced succession claim is treated as unconfirmed.
